data.title: SCHD: When The AI 'Flash Mob' Is Not For You
data.description:
The Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (NYSEARCA:SCHD) is increasingly popular (total assets have zoomed to over $70 billion), yet it has significantly underperformed the market (SPY) and especially the tech-heavy Nasdaq 100 (QQQ). This report reviews the SCHD strategy, including its attractive qualities and big risks, and then concludes with my strong opinion on investing.

About SCHD (Attractive Qualities)

Lower-volatility blue-chip safety and attractive dividend growth is what comes to mind when I think of SCHD. In particular, while the market has flocked to high-growth and technology stocks (which continue to significantly outperform), SCHD presents the type of old-school high-quality strategy that has worked for many decades. And with certain popular zero-dividend growth stock corners of the market now trading at nose-bleed valuations, SCHD makes more and more sense to a lot of investors. Particularly those who have built up significant nest eggs over the years and don't feel like risking it all on popular AI "flash mobs" like Palantir (PLTR) and Tesla (TSLA), to name a couple (more on this later).

According to the Schwab website, the objective of SCHD is to track the total return of the Dow Jones U.S. Dividend 100™ Index, which is designed to basically:

"measure the performance of high-dividend-yielding stocks in the U.S. with a record of consistently paying dividends, selected for fundamental strength relative to their peers, based on financial ratios."

So basically, SCHD owns "quality" high-dividend stocks, which is essentially the holy grail for many (but certainly not all) investors.

And to add some additional color, here are a few of the attractive qualities of SCHD that are compelling to many investors:

3.7% dividend yield: SCHD's dividend yield is nearly double the 1.9% of the S&P 500, and to a lot of investors this is an indication of safety as the companies within SCHD actually have the free cash flow to support payments (and that's more than you can say for a lot of high-growth stocks currently paying zero dividend because they are speculatively pouring everything back into their businesses just to support their very high current valuations).

Low Beta and Low Volatility are two more attractive qualities. The S&P 500 has a beta of 1.0 (by definition), and securities with betas below one (such as SCHD's 3-year beta of 0.78) are an indication of safety and low volatility.

Outperforms when the market sells off: You can also see in the earlier price performance chart that when the market sells off hard, SCHD tends to sell of less hard (i.e. keep more of what you've got), especially as compared to the S&P 500 and the Nasdaq 100.

Dividend Growth is another indication of financial strength, and as you can see in the chart below, SCHD has been doing very well in this category (as the blue chip companies it owns continue to deliver).

Not an AI Flash Mob: Artificial Intelligence ("AI") is the most popular theme in the market today, considered a long-term mega trend by many. However, as we have seen over and over throughout history, many of the most popular stocks of the day can climb rapidly (in "flash mob" type fashion) and then fall rapidly. We certainly saw this during COVID (where work-from-home stocks like Zoom (ZM) and Teladoc (TDOC) did very well very quickly and then gave back all their gains very quickly too). Currently, some people believe AI stocks like Palantir (PLTR) and Nebius (NBIS) fall into this category (SCHD has no position in any of these zero-dividend names).

Instant Diversification: with just over 100 positions, SCHD provides investors some instant diversification. Rather than racking your brain constantly worrying about what to buy or sell, SCHD does all the work for you and stays fully invested too (to help those big, growing dividends keep rolling in, while the share price is also positioned to keep climbing over the long term).

Qualified Dividends are another attractive SCHD quality. Specifically, the stocks SCHD holds typically qualify for the lower dividend taxation rate (0%, 15%, or 20%, depending on your tax bracket). This helps boost your after-tax return, something REITs, BDCs, and CEFs are typically not able to do (their distributions tend to often be taxed at your ordinary tax rate, which is typically higher and thereby reduces your after-tax returns if you own them in a taxable account).

No Gimmicky Leverage or ROC: Additionally, SCHD doesn't source distributions from return of capital ("ROC"), something that can create problems for CEFs, for example. Further still, SCHD doesn't layer on additional layers of leverage (or borrowed money) to juice the returns like some strategies do.

No Unnecessary Credit Risk: As mentioned, SCHD invests in high-quality businesses, and this is a good thing at a time when credit spreads are near historical lows (meaning investors may not currently be appropriately compensated for taking on additional risks). For example, BDCs and CEFs add a little extra yield by adding a lot of additional risk as compared to what SCHD does. SCHD's high-quality blue chip investments are attractive.

Who Might Want to Invest

SCHD is for winners. In particular, if you have already built up a significant nestegg, you may not need to take on the same high level of risk as younger investors, who still have a lot of time to make up for losses if something goes wrong.

Further, there is never any good reason to take on additional risks just to keep up with the Joneses (something SCHD does not do). For a lot of investors, they simply don't need to be chasing speculative zero-profit growth stocks at this point in the cycle and at this point in their lives.

Rather, if you are looking for steady, growing income and some healthy long-term capital appreciation, SCHD may be right for you.

Risks

Of course there are risks to investing in SCHD that investors need to be aware of.

Opportunity Cost: Perhaps, the largest risk is opportunity cost. Specifically, by omitting speculative high-growth stocks, SCHD has delivered lower returns over the long-term. This is a trend that might reverse, but it might also continue to work against SCHD investors. SCHD is less diversified than the S&P 500.

Market risk is another SCHD risk. SCHD can lose value (and it has over certain periods of time, although the long-term trend remains significantly to the upside). However, the fund's outsized dividends help pad any near-term market declines (both psychologically and from a lower price-volatility standpoint too).

Conclusion

SCHD is absolutely NOT for everyone. For example, if you are a young buck with many decades before you retire, avoiding SCHD (and instead investing in the complete S&P 500 or Nasdaq 100 stocks) may be the right strategy for you.

However, if you are at a point in life where you've already built up a significant nest egg, SCHD may be exactly right for you in terms of steady, growing qualified dividend income and long-term price appreciation (to help keep you ahead of inflation, and then some).

At the end of the day, you need to do what is right for you. Everyone's individual situation is different. And disciplined, goal-focused long-term investing continues to be a winning strategy. Be smart people.

data.title: Rate Cuts In Focus: Diverging Data And The Politics Of Monetary Policy
data.description:
By Seema Shah, Chief Global Strategist

Markets are grappling with a complex and often contradictory economic landscape. As Chair Powell noted in his speech at the 2025 Jackson Hole Economic Symposium, growth indicators remain broadly resilient, but labor market data are beginning to show signs of strain and downside risks are rising. At the same time, while not surging, the effects of tariffs on inflation are now visible and could result in a more persistent inflation dynamic. Against this tense backdrop of worrisome labor market developments and sticky inflation concerns, Chair Powell has now all but confirmed a Federal Reserve rate cut in September. Yet, the macroeconomic case for aggressive monetary easing remains debatable.

With both sides of the Fed’s dual mandate drifting from target and political pressures mounting, the size of the central bank’s cut next month could prove pivotal—not just for the economy, but also for investor sentiment and asset valuations.

Evolving labor market dynamics

The July jobs report reignited concerns about the labor market’s underlying health. While survey data had been signaling a slowdown in labor demand since late 2024, the modest 73,000 payroll gain—coupled with sharp downside revisions that pulled the three-month average to just 35,000—pushed slowdown fears to the fore. Historically, such levels have coincided with rising unemployment and heightened recession risk, prompting many investors today to reassess the labor market’s strength. Still, the story is more complex than the headline figures suggest.

The slight uptick in July’s still-low unemployment rate suggests that labor supply has slowed nearly in tandem with labor demand, keeping the overall market—as Chair Powell described in his Jackson Hole speech—in a “curious kind of balance.” As such, the disappointing payroll figures may not signal outright labor market deterioration but rather reflect an economy that requires fewer new jobs to maintain stable employment levels.

Furthermore, as the softening in labor demand has not been accompanied by a rise in layoffs or a pullback in spending, investors and policymakers should be wary of interpreting payrolls softness as an imminent recessionary signal.

Inflation calm is unlikely to last

Recent inflation developments offer little reassurance. While July’s inflation report wasn’t alarmingly strong, underlying signals point to building price pressures amid higher trade tariffs. Business surveys increasingly reflect rising cost dynamics, and producer price data suggest that inflation concerns could intensify in the months ahead.

In fact, producer price inflation in July rose at its fastest monthly pace since March 2022, signaling that businesses are already contending with higher tariff-driven input costs. While the relatively subdued CPI print suggests that firms are currently absorbing these pressures within their margins, that dynamic is unlikely to be sustainable. Eventually, companies may be forced to pass these costs on to consumers, pushing inflation higher and further away from the Fed’s 2% target.

It’s also important to note that the latest round of higher tariffs—35% on Canadian goods, 25% on Indian imports (which are set to climb to 50% on August 27), and 15% on products from the EU, Japan, and Korea—only took effect in August. As these measures filter through supply chains, they are likely to exert additional upward pressure on input costs. Consequently, the late summer and autumn inflation reports, along with business surveys, may reveal further signs of price acceleration, adding to concerns about the inflation outlook.

The politicalization of monetary policy

Both sides of the Fed’s dual mandate—maximum employment and price stability—are moving further away from target, yet markets have dramatically raised expectations for a September cut—now supported by Chair Powell’s dovish Jackson Hole speech. With the July jobs report now reinforcing the signals from earlier labor market surveys, investors appear undeterred by underlying labor supply constraints or early signs of tariff-induced price pressures. They are focused on the prospects for imminent Fed easing.

Historically, once the unemployment rate begins to rise, it often accelerates in a non-linear fashion. This lends credibility to FOMC members’ concerns that waiting for clearer signs of labor market deterioration before cutting rates could result in action that is too late to be effective. At the same time, however, recent experience has underscored the dangers of allowing inflation expectations to become unanchored, which could lead to more persistent inflationary pressures taking root. The fact that Fed rates are very likely to be reduced in September, despite inflation risks, suggests that another underlying dynamic may be influencing expectations: politics.

President Trump’s continued criticism of Chair Powell—combined with the nearing end of Powell’s term and speculation about his successor—has likely sharpened the focus of several current FOMC members (and potential future ones) on the case for rate cuts. Those seen as contenders for the Fed Chair role have a clear incentive to signal dovishness, effectively “auditioning” through their policy stance and public messaging.

While current economic indicators may not fully justify an immediate rate cut, persistent pressure from the Trump administration—and the resulting politicization of monetary policy—suggests that the Fed’s decision-making is being influenced not only by macroeconomic data but also by the broader political context shaping expectations.

Market implications

Recent months have delivered a stream of noisy and sometimes contradictory economic signals. Stepping back, growth data remain broadly resilient, the labor market is cooling but not at a pace historically consistent with recession, and inflationary pressures threaten to build. Yet despite this backdrop, an interest rate cut at the next FOMC meeting is highly likely. In effect, the interest rate outlook has diverged from the macro fundamentals—creating a supportive environment for risk assets.

However, the Fed is walking a fine line. While the case for easing has strengthened, there is little economic justification for an emergency-sized 50-basis point cut. Should the Fed opt for such a move, markets may interpret it as a sign of political influence rather than data-driven decision-making. This could push inflation expectations and term premia higher, driving long-end yields up and undermining the very conditions that have supported risk assets. With valuations already stretched, the risk of a near-term market pullback would rise meaningfully. Markets may welcome a 25-basis point cut in September—but anything more could backfire.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: BDC Weekly Review: Keep An Eye On Valuation-Adjusted Performance
data.description:
Welcome to another installment of our BDC Market Weekly Review, where we discuss market activity in the Business Development Company [BDC] sector from both the bottom-up, highlighting individual news and events-as well as the top-down-providing an overview of the broader market.

We also try to add some historical context as well as relevant themes that look to be driving the market or that investors ought to be mindful of. This update covers the period through the third week of August.

Market Action

BDCs underperformed on the week, falling around 0.5% on average. Month-to-date, performance has largely followed earnings results.

The median historic valuation remains well below its historic average level.

Market Themes

We have now processed Q2 earnings for most BDCs in our coverage. One of our favorite metrics to track across the sector is valuation-adjusted total NAV return which is just the total NAV return for a given period dividend by the company's valuation. So, taking CSWC as an example, its total NAV return for the quarter was 3.3% while its valuation is 135% (i.e. a 35% premium). This results in a 2.4% valuation-adjusted Q2 total NAV return.

The reason this metric is valuable is because it combines both valuation and performance. Plus, it captures what investors would actually generate on $1 of invested capital since that dollar will buy a different amount of NAV for each BDC. As we like to say, you can't buy the total NAV return.

If we plot this metric we get the following chart.

The chart tells us two key things. One, underperformers are typically not worth pursuing as their valuation is often not low enough for their performance. For example, consistent underperformers like HRZN, FSK, and TCPC remain value traps - there is no valuation which will make negative total NAV returns attractive. Maybe one day these companies will sort out their issues, but for now they continue to disappoint investors.

The second thing that the chart shows is that high-flyers like CSWC, MAIN, and even respected BDCs like BXSL and ARCC either underperformed on a valuation-adjusted basis or came in line with the sector average. This is because while their total NAV returns were strong, their high valuation significantly discounted that return as well.

Obviously, a single quarter is not the be-all and end-all of BDC analysis so it's worth looking at this metric across longer time periods. However, the key lesson here is that investors should consider a given company's return on a valuation-adjusted basis rather than blindly allocate to stocks that appear cheap or those that put up strong headline numbers.

Market Commentary

Main Street Capital (MAIN) priced an offering of $350m 5.4% bonds with a 2028 maturity. As usual for BDC OTC bonds, they will have a make-whole provision. This bond is likely intended to refinance a 7.74% bond that is maturing at the end of this year.

Recall that bond refinancing has been fairly painful for BDCs over the last few years. They have been replacing bonds issued during 2020-2021 that were placed at rock bottom coupons with bonds that were issued at significantly higher coupons after the rate rise in 2022. This has created a drag on BDC interest expense that has fully offset the benefit of 4 Fed rate cuts on their floating-rate credit facilities.

At this point, however, bond yields are relatively low in the context of the last 3 years and so some BDCs are able to refinance 3-year bonds that were issued in 2022 with new bonds at lower coupons. Although Treasury yields have not fallen very much over this period, credit spreads are at their record lows this century. This is one of the few bright spots for BDC net income.

The Blue Owl Technology Finance Corp (OTF) delivered a strong result with a 2.6% total NAV return. Although this is in line with the median so far (out of 21 BDCs whose earnings have been processed), it is well above the median on a valuation-adjusted basis, above stalwarts like ARCC and BXSL. The next lock-up expiry is 9-Dec. The stock looks quite tempting at its 15% discount despite potential future volatility in December.

Carlyle Secured Lending (CGBD) had a so-so quarter with a 1.2% total NAV return. On a valuation-adjusted basis, that’s 1.5% vs. an average of 1.9% so far. NII fell slightly and non-accruals rose. Performance has been below the company’s historic trend; however, it’s far too early to write it off. Stock trades at a 14% discount, 4% wider than the median.

Stance And Takeaways

There was a question this week on the service why we continue to find the BDC sector attractive despite the downward trajectory of net investment income. There are three reasons. One, is the fact that valuation in the sector is well below its historic average level (particularly on a median basis). Many high-performing BDCs are trading at or near double-digit discounts. This creates a margin of safety for investors and boosts the earnings yield on NAV. The same can't be said of the CEF sector in aggregate.

Two, as always, you want to consider your alternatives. The median BDC is delivering something like 11.2% NII on price. That will likely drop towards 10% if the Fed delivers 4 more rate cuts. There aren't very many income assets out there that will genuinely earn 10.7% over the next couple of years (averaging 11.2% and 10.2%).

There are a few bonds (which offer little diversification against a BDC with 50-300 holdings) and there are CLO Equity CEFs that produce this level of income. Other sectors don't deliver that much "income". There are CEFs that pay this out either via ROC or promised capital gains, but they don't deliver that in income. We are big holders of bonds with yields around 8-10%, however, there are not very many of those and of those we like are at or close to our allocation limits.

And finally, the yield curve is still fairly flat which is unusual by historic norms. This means that assets anchored by short-term rates like BDCs are paying out more relative to longer-term rates than they usually do. This makes the short-end of the curve still appealing in our view.

data.title: Weekly Commentary: Discounting The Loss Of Fed Independence
data.description:
The Federal Reserve is under assault. Our nation, as we have known, is under assault. Equities, well, they seemingly couldn't be chippier.

Bloomberg: "Wall Street Gets the Rally Signals From Powell It Was Hoping For." The Friday rally saw the Nasdaq Bank Index jump 4.5%, the Dow Transports 3.3%, the KBW Bank Index 3.2%, the Semiconductors (SOX) 2.7%, the Bloomberg MAG7 Index 2.5%, the Broker/Dealers Index 2.0%, and the S&P 500 1.5%. The ARK Innovation ETF jumped 4.1%. The Goldman Sachs Short Index surged 3.4%.

Friday's "may warrant adjusting" triggered a decent squeeze and unwind of hedges, in a replay of the market's response to Powell's "time has come" 2024 Jackson Hole speech. An explicit signal of looser monetary policy on August 23, 2024, saw the S&P 500 gain 1.1%, MAG7 1.8%, the Semiconductors 2.8%, the KBW Bank Index 2.4%, the Broker/Dealers 1.8%, the small cap Russell 2000 3.2%, the Goldman Sachs Short Index 3.7%, and the ARK Innovation ETF 4.5%.

It's worth noting that the S&P 500 has returned 17.6% since "time has come", the Banks 37.3%, Broker/Dealers 59.0%, MAG7 35.2%, Goldman Sachs Short Index 24.4%, and the Nasdaq100 21.5%. ARK has returned 72.9%. Notable one-year returns include Nvidia 44%, (Micro)Strategy 168%, Palantir 141%, and Tesla 61%.

Political, economic, and geopolitical backdrops have changed so momentously in a year. Market dynamics, not so much. If anything, Bubble markets have turned only more speculative, ensuring a keen fixation on financial conditions. Markets rallied a year ago on the prospect of the Fed slashing rates despite already loose financial conditions, strong Credit growth and highly speculative markets. Markets rallied Friday on confidence in more of the same.

At this market juncture, it doesn't take much to trigger squeezes. The risk attentive (who hedge and short) have been burned so many times they operate in the markets with weak (singed) hands. They are easily targeted, especially at key events (i.e., Jackson Hole, CPI, Non-Farm Payrolls) where potential bearish scenarios are avoided. Shooting fat bear in a barrel.

"May warrant adjusting" was actually less dovish and more "Balanced Powell." The rates market Monday was pricing an 85% probability of a 25 bps September rate cut, and ended the week at 81%.

Powell: "In the near term, risks to inflation are tilted to the upside, and risks to employment to the downside-a challenging situation. When our goals are in tension like this, our framework calls for us to balance both sides of our dual mandate. Our policy rate is now 100 bps closer to neutral than it was a year ago, and the stability of the unemployment rate and other labor market measures allows us to proceed carefully as we consider changes to our policy stance. Nonetheless, with policy in restrictive territory, the baseline outlook and the shifting balance of risks may warrant adjusting our policy stance. Monetary policy is not on a preset course."

Last year's "time has come" speech delivered a five bps 10-year Treasury yield decline, to 3.80%. The Treasury market's loose "money" enthusiasm was short-lived. Yields were at 3.90% a week later; 4.29% to close October; and 4.57% to end 2024.

Ten-year Treasury yields declined seven bps Friday, to 4.26%. It will be interesting to see how long bond market warmth for loosey-goosey lasts. And how long will the bond market accommodate the administration's moves to seize control of the Federal Reserve? As the Chair spoke in Jackson Hole, President Trump threatened to fire one of Powell's fellow Fed Governors.

August 22 - CNBC (Kevin Breuninger): "President Donald Trump said Friday he will fire Federal Reserve Governor Lisa Cook if she does not resign from her position. 'What she did was bad,' the president told reporters… 'So I'll fire her if she doesn't resign,' he said… If Trump were to successfully remove Cook 'for cause,' he would get the chance to reshape the central bank's governing board, potentially for years to come. Two of the seven current governors, Christopher Waller and Michelle Bowman, are Trump appointees. Both dissented from the Fed's most recent decision to hold interest rates steady. Another seat opened up earlier this summer, when Adriana Kugler announced she would step down. If Trump is able to remove Cook, he would appoint her successor - potentially ensuring that a majority of the board shares his view on monetary policy. Board members serve 14-year terms."

Spare us the histrionics over allegations of mortgages designated for a primary residence that might not have served as the borrower's primary residence for the required period (typically one year). This "bad" "mortgage fraud" has resulted in the borrower paying somewhat smaller monthly mortgage payments. It seems resources would be better spent prosecuting scores of deadbeat borrowers who fraudulently default on loans and mortgages.

August 20 - Axios (Avery Lotz): "Federal Reserve governor Lisa Cook is the latest target of one of the Trump administration's new pathways to pursue its perceived enemies: alleged mortgage fraud. Trump has waged a revenge tour from his seat in the White House, with prominent members of his grudge list now facing probes from a MAGA-aligned DOJ. If the accusation against Cook evoked a sense of déjà vu, that's because the Trump administration is also investigating Sen. Adam Schiff (D-Calif.) and New York Attorney General Letitia James (D) on similar grounds. Driving the news: Federal Housing Finance Agency Director Bill Pulte, a Trump ally, referred Cook to the DOJ over allegations she 'falsified bank documents and property records to acquire more favorable loan terms, potentially committing mortgage fraud under the criminal statutes.'"

August 20 - Wall Street Journal (Editorial Board): "Federal Housing Finance Agency (FHFA) director William Pulte… accused Federal Reserve Governor Lisa Cook, a Biden appointee, of mortgage fraud. President Trump then demanded her resignation. Where this goes is hard to know, but it's an ominous turn in political lawfare… Citing Mr. Pulte's allegations, Mr. Trump demanded on Truth Social that she 'resign now!!!' Mr. Pulte then tweeted that 'the President has great cause to fire Lisa Cook' and mused that Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell might 'be complicit with Cook's alleged fraud.' He reiterated his insinuations that Mr. Powell is covering up Ms. Cook's alleged fraud in a CNBC interview. This is nasty business. Mr. Powell doesn't have the authority to remove Ms. Cook, and why would he know anything about her mortgages? The context for Mr. Pulte's accusations is relevant. Mr. Trump is angry that the Fed hasn't cut interest rates. Ms. Cook voted with Mr. Powell to stand pat at last month's meeting of the Federal Open Market Committee."

August 20 - Bloomberg (Christopher Condon and Katy O'Donnell): "Federal Reserve Governor Lisa Cook signaled her intention to remain at the central bank, defying calls for her resignation by President Donald Trump over allegations of mortgage fraud. 'I have no intention of being bullied to step down from my position because of some questions raised in a tweet,' Cook said… 'I do intend to take any questions about my financial history seriously as a member of the Federal Reserve and so I am gathering the accurate information to answer any legitimate questions and provide the facts.'"

President Trump: "We called him too late for a reason. He should have cut them a year ago. He's too late." Well, the Fed cut rates 50 bps more than 11 months ago - 100 bps over the past year.

The Dollar Index dropped 1% Friday to a four-week low of 97.716, ominously reversing a recent rally attempt. While the stock market remains enamored by loose "money" and so far unbothered by Washington's shenanigans, international investors appear less tolerant of both.

Ten-year Treasury yields ended the week down six bps to 4.25%. Don't assume the "all's clear" has sounded. Treasury yields traded to 4.35% in Thursday afternoon trading. After Monday's pullback, European bond yields were back on the march by Tuesday. German 30-year yields rose to 3.36% in Wednesday trading, a new high back to 2011. French yields traded to 4.34% in early Friday trading, also a high since 2011. Japan's 30-year yield traded to a record high 3.21% in Friday trading, while 10-year JGB yields rose five bps this week to 1.63% - a new high back to 2008.

Future readers often occupy the back of my mind. At least there won't be a TDS diagnosis associated with my analysis. I can relate to them (to you). A decade from now - or decades and even a century ahead - future historians and students of history might stumble across my weekly posts. They will undoubtedly be curious, intrigued and likely befuddled - as I have been reading contemporaneous accounts of the "Roaring Twenties." How could everyone have believed it all? How could they have disregarded so much - remaining oblivious to the Bubble right to the end? This felt like a notable week - the believing, the disregarding, the power of Bubble excess to so distort perceptions of reality. It will appear obvious in post-Bubble hindsight.

"Is the A.I. Sell-Off the Start of Something Bigger?" "Is the Artificial-Intelligence Winter Finally Upon Us?" "Meta Freezes AI Hiring After Blockbuster Spending Spree." "AI Slurs Are Just the Start of the Backlash." "Private Credit-Powered AI Boom at Risk of Overheating, UBS Says." "Default Warnings Start to Pile Up in the Private Credit Market." "Leveraged Loan Default Rate Ticked Up to 5.2% in July: Fitch." "The Nationalization of Intel?" "Trump's Attacks on Fed Overshadow a Critical Moment for the Central Bank." "Trump's Crackdown in DC Leaves Residents on Edge as Federal Agents Set Up Checkpoints." "National Guard Troops From Six Red States Head to DC to Help Tackle Crime." "Trump Eyes National Guard Expansion Into New York, Chicago." "MAGA Celebrates John Bolton's Home Being Raided: 'How Does It Feel?'" "Putin's Ukraine Summitry Was a Big Con."

August 25 - Bloomberg (Myles Miller, Miranda Davis and Georgia Hall): "President Donald Trump said Friday he's preparing to expand federal deployments of the National Guard beyond Washington, DC, with Chicago and New York among the cities under review. The president has already moved the DC's Police Department under federal control and ordered about 2,000 troops to patrol the nation's capital. In the Oval Office on Friday, he said his next steps could involve other large, Democratic-led cities he has repeatedly criticized for crime and mismanagement."

August 25 - CNBC (Dan Mangan): "The law enforcement investigation of former Trump national security advisor John Bolton is 'in the very early stages,' Vice President JD Vance said on the heels of the FBI's raid of Bolton's Maryland home on Friday morning. Vance, in a new interview with NBC News' Kristen Welker, said that classified documents are 'certainly part of' that probe, but that 'there's a broad concern about Ambassador Bolton.' Bolton is not being targeted because of his criticism of President Donald Trump, Vance told Welker…"

John Bolton has been a distinguished public servant back to the Reagan administration. An outspoken and controversial hard-line conservative, claims he is not being targeted for his criticism of the President are dubious. Fed independence under overt attack.

Indicative of a hasty reversal of hedges, high yield CDS dropped 15 Friday to 313 bps, the largest one-day decline since May 27th (strong gain in consumer confidence data and easing US-EU trade tensions). Meanwhile, notable curve steepening continues. More specifically, the five and 30-year Treasury yield differential widened five Friday to 112 bps - the largest term premium since October 5, 2021. Not surprisingly, but curiously, market inflation expectations (five-year "breakeven rate") jumped four bps Friday to a one-month high 2.51% - and to within three bps of the high back to April 3rd (day following "liberation day"). Gold jumped $33 Friday to $3,372, boosting one-year gains to 36%. Silver surged 2% (up 34% y-o-y) and Platinum added 0.4% (up 43%).

Meager risk premiums indicate extreme complacency. The weak dollar, steepening yield curve, higher market inflation expectations, vulnerable bond markets, and inflating precious metals prices suggest festering Monetary Disorder concerns. Exuberant markets may dismiss near-term risks, but discounting the loss of Fed independence has commenced.

For the Week:

The S&P 500 increased 0.3% (up 10.0% y-t-d), and the Dow gained 1.5% (up 7.3%). The Utilities added 0.5% (up 13.5%). The Banks jumped 3.5% (up 16.1%), and the Broker/Dealers added 0.5% (up 29.7%). The Transports advanced 2.8% (up 1.3%). The S&P 400 Midcaps jumped 2.6% (up 4.3%), and the small cap Russell 2000 surged 3.3% (up 5.9%). The Nasdaq100 declined 0.9% (up 11.8%). The Semiconductors were little changed (up 15.6%). The Biotechs increased 0.7% (up 5.7%). With bullion rising $36, the HUI gold index rose 2.8% (up 77.7%).

Three-month Treasury bill rates ended the week at 4.085%. Two-year government yields declined five bps to 3.70% (down 55bps y-t-d). Five-year T-note yields fell eight bps to 3.76% (down 62bps). Ten-year Treasury yields fell six bps to 4.25% (down 32bps). Long bond yields dipped four bps to 4.88% (up 9bps). Benchmark Fannie Mae MBS yields were down eight bps to 5.38% (down 47bps).

Italian 10-year yields declined six bps to 3.53% (unchanged y-t-d). Greek 10-year yields fell six bps to 3.36% (up 15bps). Spain's 10-year yields dipped five bps to 3.30% (up 24bps). German bund yields retreated seven bps to 2.72% (up 36bps). French yields declined five bps to 3.42% (up 23bps). The French to German 10-year bond spread widened two to 70 bps. U.K. 10-year gilt yields were little changed at 4.69% (up 13bps). U.K.'s FTSE equities index jumped 2.0% (up 14.1% y-t-d).

Market Instability Watch:

August 18 - Reuters (Naomi Rovnick and Alun John): "The spectre of U.S. stagflation is stalking global markets, causing some investors to position portfolios to dodge the potential damage that tariffs could wreak on growth and inflation in the world's dominant economy. Some 70% of global investors surveyed by BofA Global Research in early August said they expect stagflation - the combination of below trend growth and above trend inflation - in the next 12 months."

August 19 - Bloomberg (Bernard Goyder): "Options traders are increasingly nervous about a plunge in technology stocks in the coming weeks and are grabbing insurance to protect themselves from a wipeout. The tech-heavy Nasdaq 100 Index is up almost 40% since its early April plunge triggered by President Donald Trump's sweeping tariffs… Traders are 'less concerned' about a 'normal run-of-the-mill pullback' and seem to be more worried about a repeat of the April selloff, said Jeff Jacobson, head of derivative strategy at 22V Research Group, who thinks a shallower dip is more likely."

August 19 - Financial Times (Ian Smith): "Investors have warned that big economies are entering a new period of 'fiscal dominance', in which central banks are under growing pressure to keep interest rates artificially low to offset the cost of record government borrowing. The most prominent case is the US, where President Donald Trump has urged the Federal Reserve to slash rates to save billions of dollars in debt-servicing charges. But government debt loads and rising borrowing costs in countries such as the UK and Japan are also putting central banks under pressure to ease monetary policy… The combination of government debt and increased costs was 'creating enormous political incentives for governments around the world to put pressure on central banks to lower rates', said Kenneth Rogoff, a professor at Harvard and former chief economist of the IMF."

August 21 - Reuters (Mike Dolan): "Market talk may be of fiscal dominance in the U.S. debt market but Washington's startling moves to take a direct stake in its leading chipmakers look like industrial policy on overdrive - alarming some investors about where it might end. For an administration that loudly espouses a credo of deregulated free markets, that's quite a look. Donald Trump's second administration is proving anything but a 'light touch' government - pursuing high trade barriers, greater control of monetary policy, heavy-handed re-industrialization, strict immigration controls and heightened national security priorities."

Global Credit and Financial Bubble Watch:

August 18 - Bloomberg (Olivia Fishlow): "Private credit lenders, and their deep pockets, are rapidly becoming an important source of capital for artificial intelligence development. That's raising concerns at UBS Global Research. As private credit grows beyond its roots of lending to smaller, heavily indebted companies, large-scale tech firms have started to see the asset class as a way to fulfill their growing capital needs. Private debt had about $450 billion loaned to the technology sector as of early 2025, up $100 billion from 12 months earlier, according to UBS estimates. For business development companies - or funds that hold direct corporate loans - tech lending has nearly doubled to $150 billion from $80 billion… Microsoft Corp., Amazon.com Inc., Google-parent Alphabet Inc., and Meta Platforms Inc. plan to spend over $344 billion this year, mainly driven by AI. At OpenAI, Chief Executive Officer Sam Altman is looking for new ways to bankroll the trillions he expects to spend on AI data center construction."

August 21 - Bloomberg (Kat Hidalgo): "Warnings on defaults are starting to pile up in the $1.7 trillion private credit market, prompting at least some analysts to raise concern about underappreciated risks in one of Wall Street's favorite money spinners. For years, losses have been contained by the fact that private credit firms have more room than many other investors to be patient with borrowers when times are tough. During the pandemic direct lenders granted companies more time to pay their obligations, often by negotiating behind the scenes with private equity owners. But a number of analysts this month are holding up a light to underlying stress… While there is no universal definition of what constitutes default in private credit, default rates for the market are currently in the 2% to 3% range. If so-called non-accrual loans… are added to the mix, that rate climbs to 5.4%, according to a JPMorgan... report…"

August 18 - Financial Times (Lee Harris): "Apollo has hit back against criticism of the close ties between its asset management arm and in-house insurer in an investor filing that instead points the finger at its chief rivals. A presentation published by Apollo's in-house insurer Athene last week zeroed in on life insurers owned by or affiliated with KKR, Blackstone and Brookfield… The presentation showed 12% of Athene's assets were 'related party' ones. That compared with 22% for KKR's Global Atlantic, 30% for Brookfield's American National Insurance, 35% for Blackstone's Everlake and 43% for Security Benefit Life, the insurer owned by the holding company of billionaire sports investor Todd Boehly."

August 20 - Bloomberg (John Cheng): "Japan's 20-year government bond yields climbed to fresh multi-decade highs, driven by persistent concerns over fiscal expansion and fading demand from key investors. Meanwhile, yields on 30-year notes rose to 3.18%, approaching the all-time peak of 3.2% seen in July."

August 20 - Financial Times (William Sandlund): "Issuance of renminbi debt outside mainland China is heading for a record high this year… Offshore issuance of renminbi debt - known as 'dim sum' bonds because most are issued in Hong Kong, where the Cantonese cuisine is popular - has hit Rmb475bn ($66.3bn) so far in 2025, on track to surpass last year's record high, as issuers take advantage of borrowing costs near record lows."

August 21 - Bloomberg (Julia Zhong, Wenjin Lv, and Shulun Huang): "A weak auction sent a fresh warning signal in China's bond market… The Ministry of Finance sold a new 30-year special sovereign note at an average yield of 2.15% on Friday, the highest since December… At the same auction, it also sold 10-year paper at 1.83%, the highest since November…"

data.title: TECS: Maybe It's Time To Be Bear On Tech
data.description:
If we grasp how we can understand whether

Financial instruments are all designed. Designed to give us exposure to a particular set of circumstances. This is as with any other tool of course. They're trying to do a specific task.

Whether we want that task done is a slightly different question. But if we grasp how the tool is constructed then we can gain a guide to how well it's going to work at the whatever it is. So, we need to spend a little time on just that, the internal construction of a financial tool.

Direxion Daily Technology Bear 3X Shares ETF (NYSEARCA:TECS)

This is an exchange traded fund. This means that it is an open ended fund - if we buy our money goes directly into the fund and the fund itself then increases its position in the whatever. Well, they keep a cash margin but that's obvious. This also means that when we sell our money comes out.

That can cause a problem - if we all arrive at the same time then the fund will have to sell positions - that cash margin will be pretty small - in order to repay. If those positions in that underlying whatever are illiquid then they might have to sell quickly at firesale prices. That makes the fund decline in value, perhaps sparking more redemptions and thus we get into a redemption cascade. This is what happened at the Woodford fund, in London for example.

This is something to consider with open-ended funds. There have been a couple of disasters in commercial property for example - perhaps not something we should invest in via an ETF therefore.

Here, with the use of options on major stocks and so on no, it's not going to be a problem.

On the other hand an ETF is very rarely going to move more than fractions of a percentage point off net asset value. A closed end fund can and often does, some by tens of percentage points.

The aim of TECS is to give us bear - and 3x - exposure to the tech sector. It's aligned, specifically, with the Technology Select Sector Index from S&P.

The weightings are:

That's not, note, the holding of the fund. That's of the index that it judges itself against. The actual holdings of the fund are:

Cash and swaps on that index. Highly liquid stuff.

Leveraged ETFs

Whenever we talk about leveraged ETFs we have to include this:

For example, consider the example of a 3x leveraged ETF where the benchmark rises from 100 to 102 (+2%) on day-1, and falls from 102 back to 100 (-1.96%) on day-2. The benchmark index is back to its original level of 100, so has flat performance.

For the leveraged ETF, however, the return would be negative. To illustrate, after the first day of trading, an initial $100 investment on a 3x leveraged ETF would rise to $106.

$100 initial + (($100 x 2% index return) x 3) = $106

The next day the 3x leveraged ETF would drop 5.88% (3 x -1.96%), resulting in a value of only $99.77.

$106 - 5.88% = $99.77

Even though the benchmark index was flat, the leveraged ETF would have resulted in a loss of -0.23% over these 2 days.

$99.77 - $100 initial value = $-0.23

And this:

Leveraged and inverse ETFs typically are designed to achieve their stated performance objectives on a daily basis. Some investors might invest in these ETFs with the expectation that the ETFs may meet their stated daily performance objectives over the long term as well. Investors should be aware that performance of these ETFs over a period longer than one day can differ significantly from their stated daily performance objectives and may potentially expose investors to significant and sudden losses.

The reason we have to include all of that is because it's important.

These leveraged ETFs are not to be used - OK, are not sensible to use - to hold a long-term position. It's possible for them to work over a few days, maybe, but even then. They're about daily price movements.

There are two reasons here, the first one in that first quote. The daily reset just means that there is money dropped every day. Also, as above, volatility of prices means that the losses can mount.

The second is a little more subtle. This ETF, for example, is using swaps to gain the exposure required. Swaps are a form of derivative - the difference with options and futures doesn't matter to us here. A derivative, by definition, includes a time value. Yes, it can be in or out of the money, a long way either way. The premium to be paid will be dependent upon the volatility in price of the thing we're looking at. But, crucially, there is that time value.

Think on it. We are betting (let us just call it that for the example) that the current price of $1 will become $1.10 at some future point. Say. OK, the longer the time period we get to run that bet the more it's going to cost us - the time value. Because the longer the period of time the more the chance that the $ will become $1.10.

This then also works the other way - time value declines as time passes. So, positions based upon derivatives lose value over time. Just what happens.

Leveraged ETFs are gaining their positions through derivatives - the value declines over time.

We can actually check this here too. This Bear ETF is paired with a Bull on the same index. So:

The two should net off to zero as their positions are exactly opposite. OK, maybe net off to zero minus a little bit for fees. At 3 months they about do, add the bull to the bear and we get close to zero. YTD, one year, we're wildly, wildly out. That's why we don't use these for long-term positions.

The expense ratio here, well, 1% is a lot compared to an index fund but for a leveraged short position it's fine. Don't forget we've already insisted that this is for short term use only, that expense basis is annual. Size seems to be 250 pieces at a 1 cent bid/offer spread (while the market is open and we really should only be dealing in a leveraged ETF when it is - daily price resets, right?). We can certainly make retail sized trades in and out at low cost then.

So, what's it for then?

TECS is for short-term bear speculations on the tech sector. This means it can - and should - be used for speculative or hedging purposes. Simply not for long term position holding.

OK, so what then?

So let us talk current markets. There's a certain amount of talk around that AI is a bubble. There's going to be a crash - or, OK, a repricing. We could be cynics about this and insist that the bubble isn't over yet - because people are still talking about a bubble. It's only when it's all termed the new normal that we should start to think that the bubble is popping. But that is to be cynical. It's the sort of cute thing journalists like me say rather than what market participants do.

But, OK, talk of a considerable repricing. But of what? We might want to simply speculate - in which case TECS is a fine place to do it. We think the price reset is to happen today (say, around the results announcements of one of the major players) and want to be short this specific sector of the market - and with leverage. TECS would be great for that.

We might even want to run this the other way. We have a considerable position in one of those index components which we think will not be affected by the great repricing. So, we're not going to sell out - but we want to be sure. So, pick up some TECS as a hedge.

It's even possible that we think the sector will fall, one or more components will not (How much would Salesforce be affected by an AI bust?) and so straddle, long that component and short the index.

There are other possible uses of TECS as well. But the point to be grasped is that they are all short-term speculations or hedges. As such, obviously it's not really possible to detail actual potential trades - by the time this is published the markets will be different, as they will be in a week or three.

Why I'm wrong

Some of these leveraged ETFs have done well over the long term. The price of the sector or stock has gone in only the one direction and with little volatility. So, holders over time have done well - but always worse than if the same position had been held by other means. Those price resets, that derivatives time value, those problems don't go away.

Leveraged ETFs are for short-term positions.

My opinion

I think there is going to be some serious action in the tech stocks in the near future. As such I think TECS is going to become much more interesting. TECL, the bull, has $3.5 billion in it. TECS, the bear, $103 million. That looks unweighted to me. Simply because I do think that the AI boom will run out of steam.

The difficulty, obviously, is when, which day? That's something I don't know either. But TECS is a good tool for when we think it is about to happen - which is the point of this piece. TECS is a good speculative tool for a falling tech stock market. That's what we should use it for.

data.title: NuScale Q2 Earnings Show Progress, But Risks Still High
data.description:
Q2 2025 Results

NuScale Power Corporation (SMR) recently reported its Q2 2025 earnings. The results of the second quarter of 2025 showed some progress, but the financial situation remains difficult and resembles a classic pre-commercial technology startup - huge expenses, minimum income, and reliance on investors. Revenue was $8.1 million compared to just $1 million a year ago. This is more than 700% annual growth, but if you look at the share price after the earnings, and I bet you did that, the result still did not match analysts' forecasts, which ranged around $11–12 million. In other words, growth is impressive on paper, but in reality, the expectations remained unmet.

Profitability indicators have not changed substantially - the company is still generating losses. EPS was -$0.13, which is significantly better than -$0.31 a year ago, but still worse than forecasted -$0.11. This means that losses decrease, but more slowly than investors would like to see. Operating costs amounted to $44.9 million, up slightly from $42 million a year ago, which indicates that NuScale is actively increasing costs to move to the real commercial phase. In other words, the losses are under control, but the company is still far from profitability.

The biggest light in the financial statement that I noticed is the balance. The company still has $489.9 million in cash and cash equivalents, short-term, long-term investments, which provides more than a 2-4 year financial runway if the cost remains at the current level. Such a reserve allows the SMR to continue carrying out projects without urgent need to borrow or issuing new shares. However, in reality, if there are no strong contracts signed in the coming years, the only way to continue will be an additional issuance of shares, which means the potential risk of diluting shareholders.

It is important to note that, despite the growth of revenue, most of it comes not from the sale of modular reactors, but from design and engineering services provided through partners such as Fluor and the RoPower project in Romania. It is a temporary funding model that cannot ensure long-term stability. The real breakthrough in financial health could only occur when the construction of at least one full reactor with real payments for equipment and installation begins. Sadly, there is no such order yet—both Romanian and US projects are still stuck in the planning stages.

In conclusion, the financial situation of NuScale is ambiguous today. On one hand, the company has a strong cash reserve, which allows it to survive for a few more years. On the other hand, operating losses are not decreasing fast enough, and revenue is still symbolic. Q2 2025 results only confirmed that this company is in waiting mode: investors pay for the vision, but this vision has not yet become a cash flow. The coming year will show whether the company will be able to turn its technology into a real business or whether it will remain an expensive nuclear energy experiment.

Activities and projects

The operating reality remains the same as in recent years - much vision but little real implementation. Although the technology has already received the second design approval from the NRC (this time the 77 MWe module), which gives the regulatory advantage over competitors, the commercial path is just beginning.

The main project is RoPower. This is where Feed Stage 2 is being carried out with Fluor, but the start of construction is still unclear. The final investment decision is expected only in 2026-2027, so the next two years will be a waiting period. Other projects in Kazakhstan, South Korea, or the United States are still merely memoranda of cooperation with no binding obligations.

NuScale still does not have any ordered modules. This is a major challenge: until the first real implemented project, all technology remains unproven on the commercial side.

Guidance and management expectations

The leadership at the earnings call presentation said that the main goal of 2025 was to sign strong contracts with 2-3 clients by the end of the year. It was one of the few specific promises. CEO John Hopkins clearly stated that market interest increased after NRC approval, but investors want real contracts rather than letters of intent.

CFO Ramsey Hamady added that the second half of the year will be more active in terms of costs, the company orders the so-called long-lead items needed to prepare the production chain. This means that the costs will increase, even if the income remains modest.

The management also said that the RoPower project FID is expected in 2026–2027, so in my opinion, the next few years will still be an investment rather than a return period.

Competition

In past few days, the SMR sector has received a lot of attention from Google's decision to cooperate with Kaira Power on advanced modular reactors in the U.S., although this event does not directly touch NuScale. In my opinion, increasing attention itself is a positive sign for the entire SMR sector, as it legitimizes technology as a real energy source.

Risks

NuScale’s risks remain almost the same as per my last article:

Technological Risk - Although NRC approvals provide confidence, it has not yet been proven that reactors can be built, run, and operated commercially.

Financial risks - cash is sufficient for two to four years, but if there are no orders during that time, the company will have to issue new shares. This would mean a dilution of the shares. Obviously, that is not good. But they have a tendency to do that.

Q2 2023 Q2 2024 Q2 2025
Shares ~72 million ~95 million ~133 million
Change % - +31.98% +40.15% / +84.97% vs. Q2 2023

This shows that the company has been funding itself by issuing new shares, reducing old shareholder value. If this practice continues, even if commercial progress is achieved, the profits per share will be low.

Despite the losses, the company's market capitalization in Q2 2025 was over $9 billion, more than triple YoY, this is, in my opinion, quite a growth for such low profitability. It signals that investors pay for the vision, not the facts. If, by the end of 2025, "strong" contracts are not signed, I expect the company to have a sharp correction.

Political risk is quite big as well, projects depend on national solutions (Romania, USA, Kazakhstan). One political turn may delay the project for a decade.

The risk of competition: during this time, the renewable energy (sun, wind, batteries) can become even cheaper, which will reduce the commercial attractiveness of the SMRs.

Summary

Q2 2025 showed that NuScale was taking steps forward: income increased, losses decreased, regulatory approval for the 77 MWe module gave a competitive advantage. However, the fundamental problem has not changed - there is no real order.

Investors currently pay for the vision, not for the facts. If by the end of 2025 management will actually present at least one solid contract, the sentiment may change drastically. However, if the promise is postponed again, the shares will remain speculative.

Therefore, my position remains the same - Hold. This is not the worst option for a long-term investor in the face of a revival of nuclear energy, but in the short term the risk exceeds the benefits.

data.title: Kandi Technologies Financials Continue To Show Extreme Volatility In H1 2025
data.description:
Kandi Technologies (NASDAQ:KNDI) recently reported 1H25 results, confirming the view from my initiation article that the company's financials are simply too hard to understand.

Revenues fell almost 50% YoY, with impacts on profitability and operating leverage. Despite this, the company is entering two new businesses (battery replacement station installation in China, and robot commercialization), and its cash reserves have jumped by almost $140 million since December 2024. The company hosted a scant earnings call without a Q&A session.

Despite having a market cap of a little above 0.5x net cash (cash plus restricted cash plus certificates of deposit), the name is unattractive because it is almost impossible to understand its business, financials, and whether cash will be distributed.

1H25 financials

Super hard to understand

The main point around my Hold rating when I first covered Kandi was that the company's financials changed too much from release to release, with business segments surging to then collapse, and changes between asset categories without much explanation.

This quarter is simply another chapter in that trend.

Revenues down 40%

The company's revenues fell 40% YoY, and the company's single comment about the issue on the release or the earnings call was 'mainly due to a decrease in sales of off-road vehicles and EV products.' I cannot remember a company whose revenues halve from one release to the next, and provides that little information.

The figures make sense, though, considering that the company's production is almost entirely done in China, and that therefore its products now face much higher tariffs. It is probable that retailers in the US that carry Kandi products are trying to search for alternatives.

Little effect on earnings, with nuances

Generally, if a manufacturer or reseller (Kandi is a mix of the two) sees revenues fall by 40%, then I would expect a significant decrease in earnings, because of operational leverage. In contrast to this, Kandi's business was almost unscathed or even better.

Gross profits fell only 14%. These either imply that the missing 40% of revenues provided very little in terms of gross profit, or that the margins are non-recurring. I believe this second factor is more important, because the company said on the release that 'The improvement in gross margin was primarily driven by changes in product mix and regional revenue distribution, as well as increased sales of impaired inventory compared with the same period last year.' The last point is important. Last year, the company impaired inventory, charging it in G&A. If the company sold that article this year, the gross margin is probably close to 100%, because that product had already been written down.

That is, it is probable that the company's gross profits, based on actual inventory costs, are much lower. Then we have OpEx, which seems to be down YoY, from $23 million to $18 million. However, this is in part also driven by impairment last year recorded in G&A, and not adjusted as most companies do, to show more recurring earnings capacity.

The result is that operational losses actually improved from about $4.4 million last year to $2 million this year.

Going into new ventures

Normally, a company suffering in one of its most important segments would try to focus on restructuring, focusing on the core, and turn that business around, unless it was considered unsalvageable for some reason.

It is not the case of Kandi, who can see revenue lines come and go with ease. I wrote in my last article: 'For example, sales in China moved from about $52 million in FY22 to $30 million in FY23 and back to $53 million in FY24. That is -43% and +76% in consecutive years. The opposite happened in the US, with revenues jumping 42% ($28 million) from FY22 to FY23 and then falling by 20% (~$20 million) in FY24. (...) Product lines also move a lot over time. EV parts, which represented almost $9 million in FY22, were no longer generating any meaningful revenue in FY24. The same can be said of lithium-cells, from $24 million to about $4 million in the same period.'

This time, the company did not publish revenue by lines (or complete financials for that matter), but it announced that it is entering two new business lines. One is the supply of heavy-truck battery swap equipment to CATL (OTC:CTATF), arguably the largest battery company in the world.

It has also entered the business of 'embodied intelligence' or robots, but without much detail provided, only the comment that it is developing dog-robots, and potentially gold caddy robots for the NA market, in conjunction with Deep Robotics, an established Chinese robotics manufacturer.

New (old) changes in the balance sheet

In my original article on the name, I called attention to $144 million in receivables, which have appeared on the balance sheet from one year to the next (FY23, $6 million), and for which the 20-F provided little explanation.

This quarter, just like they appeared, they disappeared. The receivables were converted to certificates of deposit, and therefore, the cash and equivalents, restricted cash, and CD lines for the company jumped by almost $140 million between releases, to $256 million, or almost 2x its current market cap ($140 million).

The company did not publish complete financials with the SEC, and therefore, I cannot access a detailed cash flow statement. Still, I should warn people bullish on the company's cash holdings that they jump around from release to release, without a clear relation to the business operations.

Valuation is irrelevant without clarity

The company arguably trades at a deep value valuation, with $256 million in cash, restricted cash, and CDs, $70 million in other current assets, against $87 million in total liabilities. That is a net current assets balance of $240 million, against a market cap of $140 million.

However, with the financials jumping all over the place, the company is generating operating losses (potentially much worse than in the release earnings because of the gross profit lift of selling impaired inventory), sales falling 40% while the company enters two new businesses on which it has no experience, and no comments about capital allocation priorities (no Q&A allowed on the earnings call), the name is simply too risky.

The value in a deep value company is that it can still remain profitable, potentially pay high dividends with profits and its balance sheet, and maybe even liquidate some of the excess cash. In this case, none of those positions is tenable, and therefore the name is clearly not an opportunity.

I maintain a Hold rating on Kandi Technologies.

Market review and outlook

The world equity markets rebounded from initial weakness to register strong gains in the second quarter. Stocks fell sharply in early April in response to U.S. tariff announcements on concerns that Washington’s new approach to trade would lead to slower economic growth. Within a week, however, the U.S. administration reversed course and declared a 90-day pause on tariffs. The move fueled hopes that the initial plan was largely a negotiating tactic, providing investors with confidence that trade policy would ultimately be less restrictive than first thought. In addition, data related to global growth, inflation, and corporate earnings exhibited stability despite the shifting policy backdrop.

Equities recovered all of their earlier losses in the ensuing rally, propelling the major world indexes to all-time highs by the end of June. Growth stocks, lower- quality companies, and the mega-cap U.S. technology sector led the way higher, while the value style and defensive investments generally underperformed. This marked a pronounced reversal from the trends in place during the first quarter.

Bonds also delivered positive total returns. After experiencing volatility early in the quarter, the fixed-income markets recovered on the strength of low inflation, moderate growth, and expectations that central banks would continue to ease monetary policy. Credit-oriented market segments performed well in this environment and generally outpaced government debt.

Contributors and detractors

Asset allocation contributed to results, primarily as a result of our decision to maintain an overweight in stocks relative to bonds. An underweight in emerging market stocks and an overweight in international large-cap developed stocks also made a small contribution. On the other hand, an overweight in U.S. midcaps, which did not keep pace with large caps, had a modest, negative effect on results. Underlying manager performance detracted somewhat, with nearly all of the shortfall occurring in the equity portfolio.

Portfolio changes

The first half of the year was characterized by dramatic swings in the markets, both in terms of price movements and investors’ rapid shifts among specific asset classes. For our part, we don’t attempt to time the market or chase momentum in certain areas that happen to be outperforming in a given month. Instead, we remain focused on the core tenets of our investment process: fundamentals, valuations, and diversification. While we frequently make small changes to the portfolio, the moves are based on rigorous analysis and a three- to five-year outlook. In addition, we strive to keep the portfolio positioned for a broad range of outcomes instead of trying to predict the path of the economy or the direction of government policy. We believe this steady approach, rather than one that attempts to react to the ever-changing daily headlines, is the optimal way to help the fund achieve its longer-term investment objectives.
